channel.title: Posts on Self Portrait
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Self Portrait

item.title: Reading fast and slow
item.description:
As a studious kid in school, I always looked out for ways to improve my learning efficiency. During the scramble for exam preparations, I came across a “speed reading” course. In no time I got the course material delivered home. It was a bunch of weird exercises like reading
inverted pages and reading without enunciating the words. Soon after I was almost unable to read anything at all.

Often we come across speed readers. The biography of Franklin D Roosevelt claims that he read a book every single day. I have a friend who once read a 10,000 page fan fiction of Harry Potter in 8 days. I often wonder how awesome it would be to scan through 300 pages in a couple of hours. With my speed I won’t finish all the books I want to read in my lifetime. No technique has ever helped me. Now, I have come to terms with my slow reading: very good terms.

“The tortoise inched steadily and outstripped the callous hare as it took a nap.” Some would skim through those words only to get to the next event in the story. Some would momentarily spawn a vivid visualization of it. They would have their own cinematic perspective as they witness the tortoise look back at the hare in slow motion. To slow readers like me, it is neither of these. A hundred sleeping hares from my life’s experiences emerge in front of me all at the same time. I have to choose between the literal and the figurative; stack the information in my mind’s shelves for motivation, for information and for appreciation of language. I reread to reflect reflect. I hunt my memory to check if this information is a branch of a thought tree that was growing for a long time. The pause is sometimes more than just a moment.

The attempt to read faster took me farther from myself. I have realized that my way of reading doesn’t drift with the author. Instead it is simply a process in the composition of my life’s experience. Hence a slow reader.

item.title: Faith
item.description: Faith in any form—religious faith or faith in ones own ability—is not above reason. Unwavering faith is a sharp tool, indeed. Faith leads to belief. Belief destroys doubt. This paves the way to tireless pursuits—the kind of pursuits that resulted in the man-made wonders of the world. In fact, some of the best minds invented it. Why? Both the simple minded and the intelligent feel and respond to pain. Faith is that thing that transcends this pain and fuses the person with the job in hand. Faith is an invented tool. When faith was taken to be above everything, genocides resulted. Faith needs to be in its place—its place as a tool. The intelligent mind created this tool after coming to terms with its own limits. It is consciousness that is superior to both! Now smile. :)
